data.title: Trump tells governors Supreme Court tariff decision is a 'disgrace'
data.description:
President Trump called the Supreme Court’s decision striking down a bulk of his tariffs imposed under emergency powers “a disgrace” during a private meeting with governors on Friday, a source familiar told The Hill.

The court’s decision came just as Trump was hosting governors for a breakfast at the White House. He is expected to brief reporters this afternoon from the press briefing room, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on the social platform X.

The decision came down from the high court during the meeting, marking a significant defeat for Trump, who has made tariffs a centerpiece of his economic agenda.

In a 6-3 ruling, the justices rejected Trump’s expanded use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) in imposing tariffs on nearly every country. The 1970s law allows the president to “regulate” imports when necessary to respond to national emergencies that pose an “unusual and extraordinary” threat.  

“We claim no special competence in matters of economics or foreign affairs,” Chief Justice John Roberts wrote.

“We claim only, as we must, the limited role assigned to us by Article III of the Constitution. Fulfilling that role, we hold that IEEPA does not authorize the President to impose tariffs.”

Two of Trump’s Supreme Court nominees, Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett, ruled with Roberts alongside the court’s liberals against the president’s tariff policy.

Trump is the first president to invoke the IEEPA to impose tariffs.

The president’s political opponents were quick to celebrate the decision.

“This is a win for the wallets of every American consumer,” Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said in a statement. “Trump’s chaotic and illegal tariff tax made life more expensive and our economy more unstable. Families paid more. Small businesses and farmers got squeezed. Markets swung wildly.”

Rep. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), a vocal GOP critic of Trump’s, applauded the ruling in a post on X.

“In defense of our Republic, the Supreme Court struck down using emergency powers to enact taxes,” Paul wrote. “This ruling will also prevent a future President such as AOC from using emergency powers to  enact socialism.”

White House chief of protocol Monica Crowley, meanwhile, said Trump has “plenty of tools in the tariff toolbox,” suggesting the president may seek other ways to impose the global duties.

“Don’t be a Panican,” Crowley wrote. “Trust Trump.”

data.title: Virginia judge blocks Democrats' redistricting referendum
data.description:
A Virginia judge dealt another blow on Thursday to Democrats’ redistricting efforts, blocking an April 21 referendum while the case is heard in court. 

Tazewell County Circuit Judge Jack Hurley Jr. granted a motion from the Republican National Committee that sought to block the April referendum, arguing against the timing and phrasing of the ballot question that would be posed to voters.

The emergency injunction, as shared by the Virginia Mercury, is in effect until March 18, though early voting on the referendum is scheduled to start March 6.

It’s the latest legal obstacle to Democrats’ redistricting plan in Virginia, where Democrats hold six of the state’s 11 congressional districts. Their plans for a redrawn map could give them as many as four pickup opportunities ahead of the high-stakes midterms. 

State lawmakers’ plan is to put the state constitutional amendment before voters in an April 21 referendum. If approved, the new map would temporarily take effect mid-decade, bypassing the state’s redistricting commission. 

Virginia is one of the latest states to wade into the national redistricting war as parties fight for control of Congress in the midterms. 

Republicans’ House campaign arm spokesperson Mike Marinella called the new ruling “a massive win.”

But Virginia Attorney General Jay Jones (D) said his office plans to “immediately appeal” the ruling, noting that these arguments are already before “the proper forum” in the Virginia Supreme Court.

Hurley had ruled earlier this month against the referendum in a separate matter, but the Supreme Court of Virginia this month allowed it to move forward as litigation proceeds.

data.title: Live updates: Supreme Court strikes down most of Trump's tariffs in 6-3 ruling
data.description:
The Supreme Court dealt a crushing blow to President Trump’s tariff agenda, ruling on Friday that his tariff scheme is illegal without congressional authorization. 

In one of the biggest cases justices have decided in years, the majority conservative court issued a rare rebuke of the president’s actions. Chief Justice John Roberts and Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett — the latter two appointed by Trump — joining the three liberal justices in striking down the levies that were based on an international economic emergency.

Trump will hold a press briefing on the ruling at 12:45 p.m.

Meanwhile, Trump hosted governors at the White House for breakfast Friday, but not under the umbrella of the National Governors Association (NGA), whose meeting is underway in Washington. Trump has tangled with two Democratic governors — Wes Moore of Maryland and Jared Polis of Colorado — which cast a shadow on the breakfast and Saturday night’s annual dinner. Both decided to attend the meeting, reversing course.

Follow live below.

data.title: Hegseth criticizes 'Godless left' at Christian media convention
data.description:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth criticized “Godless” left and foreign ideologies while defending “Western Christian” ideas and traditions Thursday night at a Christian communicators conference in Nashville, Tenn.

“Gone is Godless and divisive [diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI)], gone is gender-bending equity and quotas, gone is climate change worship to a false God. We are one military, one fighting force, one nation under God. We are not in woke we trust, we are in God we trust,” Hegseth said as the headline speaker of the National Religious Broadcasters 2026 International Christian Media Convention.

Hegseth used his speech to rail against what he says is “a world beset by dangerous and godless foreign ideologies that sow doubt, sin, confusion and death,” while praising the agenda of President Trump.

The Trump administration last year banned tansgender service members from the military, cut numerous climate change and DEI-related programs and sought to curtail paying for military personnel to travel outside their state to seek an abortion.

“Under the leadership of President Trump, the military once again supports and trains our troops and tends to their spiritual health. You see, we train our troops, we no longer trans our troops,” Hegseth said.

Since taking the helm of the Pentagon last year, Hegseth has upended the tradition that defense secretaries stay out of public political commentary.

He has focused instead on shaping the military’s culture in more traditional Christian conservative views, enacting policy that bans transgender troops, reviews whether women should serve in combat roles and reevaluates the Defense Department’s support for Scouting America unless it institutes “core value reforms.”  

On Thursday, he insisted “the other side is fueled by godless and toxic ideologies, foreign to the western way of life, with tolerant hearts filled with rage and hate.”

Hegseth’s address directly follows his invitation to controversial Christian nationalist pastor Doug Wilson to give a sermon at the Pentagon on Tuesday.

Wilson led a worship service in the building as part of the military’s monthly prayer meeting, begun by Hegseth back in May. 

The Pentagon chief is the Trump administration’s most prominent follower of Wilson’s, whose church — the Communion of Reformed Evangelical Churches — preaches beliefs that homosexuality should be a crime, women should submit to their husbands and the 19th Amendment granting women the right to vote should be repealed.

Hegseth referred to the monthly prayer services in his speech, claiming the left “hate it.” 

“The left wing shrieks which means we’re right over the target,” he said to applause.

The convention also included a rendition of the National Anthem sung by Alexis Wilkins, the girlfriend of FBI Director Kash Patel, and a ceremonial color guard from the 101st Airborne Division’s band, the NRB said in a press release. 

The inclusion of the band is unusual as ceremonial support by the military typically shies away from religious services.

data.title: Les Wexner's attorney whispers 'I'll f—ing kill you' for giving long answers during deposition
data.description:
Billionaire retail mogul Les Wexner’s lawyer was caught on microphone threatening to “kill” Wexner for giving long answers during a Wednesday deposition related to his involvement with sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. 

“I will f—ing kill you if you answer another question with more than five words, okay,” Wexner’s lawyer said four and a half hours into the deposition with the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee on Wednesday.

Both Wexner and his attorney briefly chuckled, Wexner appearing to stifle his laughter as the attorney told him to “answer the question.” Wexner replied “okay” and the deposition proceeded. 

Wexner, who is connected to brands like Bath & Body Works, Abercrombie & Fitch and Victoria’s Secret, has long been criticized for being connected to Epstein, who served as his financial adviser from the 1990s into the early 2000s.

Virginia Giuffre, an outspoken victim of Epstein’s who died by suicide last year, reported being trafficked to Wexner, a claim he has denied. Files made public by the Department of Justice, show Wexner as a co-conspirator in the Epstein case on a 2019 FBI document though Wexner has not been charged with any crime. 

During Wednesday’s deposition, Wexner denied having any firsthand knowledge of Epstein’s sex trafficking operation. He told the committee that Epstein lived a “double life” and conned him and his wife and stole money from their family. Upon Epstein’s guilty plea and learning of his “deception” of their family, Wexner said he cut all ties with the convicted sex offender and did not visit him while he was in jail. 

“Let me state from the start: I was naive, foolish, and gullible to put any trust in Jeffrey Epstein. He was a con man. And while I was conned, I have done nothing wrong and have nothing to hide,” he said during his opening statement at the deposition. 

“Let me be crystal clear: I never witnessed nor had any knowledge of Epstein’s criminal activity,” Wexner added.

data.title: The US still needs a strong Black press
data.description:
For hundreds of years, through slavery, post-Emancipation terror and much of Jim Crow segregation, America’s white newspapers generally either ignored African Americans’ plight or actively supported their subjugation.

Especially in the South, but also in the North, white publications from rural weeklies to national magazines cranked out inflammatory news stories and essays supporting white supremacism, calling for everything from political suppression to lynching. 

For most of two centuries, the Black press has been pivotal in fighting white supremacism, articulating it without other voices’ mediating. 

In 1827, John Brown Busswurm and Samuel Cornish established Freedom’s Journal, the first African American periodical. Abolitionist leader Frederick Douglass launched The North Star in 1847. Other free Blacks’ sporadic efforts followed — sometimes helped by white abolitionists and, later, white liberals — published in the North and later circulated hand-to-hand throughout the South.

“The Black press remained pivotal after emancipation, encouraging African Americans to migrate north and west during the early twentieth century,” said Trevy McDonald, who teaches a course on “The Black Press and U.S. History” at the University of North Carolina. “Unlike mainstream newspaper reporting,  Black publications gave voice and humanity to their communities’ experiences.” 

The most prominent and powerful publications included the Chicago Defender, circulated surreptitiously throughout the South by A. Philip Randolph’s Pullman Porters’ union, the Baltimore Afro-American and the Pittsburgh Courier. The height of their circulation saw 250 newspapers and a news service, the Associated Negro Press, which covered sensational political cases such as those of the Scottsboro Boys and Emmett Till. Martin Luther King Jr. was a syndicated columnist.

The Crisis, the NAACP’s journal, edited by W.E.B. Du Bois, highlighted African Americans’ achievements, but famously focused on early 20th century lynchings, through Walter White’s pioneering on-site reporting. Other publications followed suit, sometimes taking aggressive, provocative stands against white supremacist tropes that resulted in mob violence. 

In 1892, assailants burned to the ground the Memphis office of the Free Speech and Headlight newspaper, co-edited and co-owned by Ida B. Wells. The attack followed Wells’s editorials condemning a recent spate of lynchings and denouncing “that old threadbare lie that Negro men rape white women. If Southern men are not careful, they will overreach themselves and public sentiment will have a reaction; a conclusion might be reached which will be very damaging to the moral reputation of their women.”

Wells famously wrote: “The way to right wrongs is to shine the light of truth upon them.” 

Six years later, the precipitating spark for 1898’s Wilmington, N.C., Massacre was a strident editorial in a Black-owned Daily Record newspaper that challenged the notion that Black men had sexual designs on white women. In the subsequent white riot, the newspaper was a prime target: Its building was burned, its presses dragged into the street and destroyed. The editor, Alexander Manly, barely escaped alive. 

During the mid-20th century civil rights movement, the Black press was prepared to do its part. Writing in 1958, Martin Luther King Jr., called the Black press “the conscience of our nation. … It has become angry for people who dare not express anger themselves. It has cried for Negroes when the hurt was so great that tears could not be shed. … It has been a crusading press and that crusade has, from its beginning in 1827, been the cry of ‘Freedom.’” 

Most North American legacy newspapers today are ailing or failing, with AI greasing the skids. Earlier this month, the weekly African American Richmond Free Press announced it was closing after 30 years, citing a collapse of advertising. 

Still, some Black newspapers, like some rural white weeklies, have managed to survive the print implosion by providing local news, information and opinions simply not available in aggregated form elsewhere. They also provide training and entry-level opportunities for young Black journalists. These jobs are evaporating in legacy white media; witness recent Washington Post and Atlanta Journal Constitution layoffs.

The internet and social media have transformed both mainstream publications and the modern Black press. Black dailies and weeklies have shifted entirely or partially to digital, reaching a potential audience of more than 20 million.

“We have shown the nation the struggle, sacrifice, progress and triumph of Black Americans and of America as a nation from the Black perspective,” wrote the Rev. Benjamin Chavis, CEO of the National Newspaper Press Association, the nation’s Black press trade organization. “No other print or digital media serves in this role quite like the Black Press.” 

Mark I. Pinsky is a journalist and author based in Durham, N.C.

data.title: Gorsuch, Barrett side with liberals on nixing Trump tariffs
data.description:
Two of President Trump’s nominees to the Supreme Court joined the chief justice and the high court’s liberal flank in striking down the bulk of Trump’s sweeping tariffs, cast as the most significant economic and foreign policy initiative of his second presidency. 

Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett, whom Trump nominated to the Supreme Court during his first term in 2017 and 2020, joined the 6-3 majority in ruling that Trump’s expanded use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) in imposing tariffs on nearly every country was unlawful. 

Trump is the first president to try to impose tariffs by invoke IEEPA, a 1970s-era law that lets the president “regulate” imports when a national emergency posing an “unusual and extraordinary threat” necessitates it. 

“Whatever else might be said about Congress’s work in IEEPA, it did not clearly surrender to the President the sweeping tariff power he seeks to wield,” Gorsuch wrote in a concurring opinion. 

Barrett said in her own concurring opinion that the “most natural reading” of IEEPA does not empower presidents to impose tariffs.  

Despite aligning with the liberals on the outcome, the justices split on some of the underlying legal issues. 

The liberal justices said they would not weigh the so-called “major questions doctrine,” which holds that presidents may not enact initiatives with major economic or political implications where Congress’s intentions are vague, while the court’s conservatives sparred over how and whether it should be applied.  

“Nothing in IEEPA’s text, nor anything in its context, enables the President to unilaterally impose tariffs,” Justice Elena Kagan wrote in a concurring opinion joined by Justices Sonia Sotomayor and Ketanji Brown Jackson. “And needless to say, without statutory authority, the President’s tariffs cannot stand.” 

Though Trump has scored a series of wins before the high court since returning to the White House, the justices were dubious that he could justify his economic strategy with the string of emergencies he cited.  

Trump in February declared an emergency over fentanyl to tariff China, Canada and Mexico, and later, he cited a trade deficit emergency to impose his so-called reciprocal tariffs on dozens of trading partners across the globe.  

“Based on two words separated by 16 others in Section 1702(a)(1)(B) of IEEPA — ‘regulate’ and ‘importation’ — the President asserts the independent power to impose tariffs on imports from any country, of any product, at any rate, for any amount of time,” Chief Justice John Roberts for the majority. “Those words cannot bear such weight.” 

The levies have continued as challenges have moved through the courts, and sector-specific tariffs, like those on steel, aluminum and copper, were not at issue. 

In 2025, the U.S. brought in $289 billion in revenue from gross tariff and certain other excise taxes, according to the Bipartisan Policy Center — a $190 billion jump from 2024. This year, some $35 billion in gross tariff and certain other excise tax revenue has been brought in so far already, the center says.  

Justice Brett Kavanaugh, whom Trump picked for the high court in 2018, authored a dissenting opinion joined by Justices Clarence Thomas and Samuel Alito that said the court’s decision may not entirely foil Trump’s bid to impose sweeping tariffs, albeit under a different statutory justification.  

“In the meantime, however, the interim effects of the Court’s decision could be substantial,” Kavanaugh wrote. “The United States may be required to refund billions of dollars to importers who paid the IEEPA tariffs, even though some importers may have already passed on costs to consumers or others.  

“As was acknowledged at oral argument, the refund process is likely to be a ‘mess,’” he added.  

Hundreds of companies have already sued to protect their claims to recover the billions in now-invalidated tariffs they’ve paid. 

“In all, the legislative process helps ensure each of us has a stake in the laws that govern us and in the Nation’s future,” Gorsuch wrote. “For some today, the weight of those virtues is apparent. For others, it may not seem so obvious. 

“But if history is any guide, the tables will turn and the day will come when those disappointed by today’s result will appreciate the legislative process for the bulwark of liberty it is,” he said.

data.title: Ocasio-Cortez knocks Vance Board of Peace joke
data.description:
Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) knocked Vice President Vance for the silence that followed after he tried to rib her during President Trump’s inaugural Gaza Board of Peace meeting Thursday in Washington.

Vance first thanked Trump for his leadership before making a joke about Ocasio-Cortez after she appeared to stumble through one of her responses at the Munich Security Conference last week, when she was asked about whether the U.S. should commit troops to defend Taiwan against China.

Ocasio-Cortez also falsely referenced Venezuela as being “below the equator.”

“I knew exactly what I wanted to say, but then after the president said that I was so smart, and that I didn’t want to repeat our congresswoman who froze for 20 seconds over in Munich — now, I’m tempted, sir, just to freeze for 20 seconds and just stare at the cameras and maybe they’ll say nice things about me like they do about Congresswoman Cortez,” he said.

His nudge at the New York Democrat‘s slip-ups, however, was met with silence.

“The only thing longer than my pause to think was their silence to his joke,” the lawmaker wrote on social platform X, alongside a video of his comments.

Her remarks in Munich received mixed reactions from Democrats. While some said she did well, the Taiwan response and other comments sparked criticism from some who suggested she lacked foreign policy experience — which could be critical if she chooses to run for higher office.

Ocasio-Cortez has been floated as a potential 2028 presidential candidate. Though, she said her speech in Munich was unrelated.

Trump blasted the lawmaker for her comments at the summit, which he said was “not a good look.” Vance also previously called the speech “embarrassing.”

The president launched the Board of Peace last month and called on many nations to join, though some have rejected the invitation. The panel is tasked with overseeing execution of the U.S.-brokered 20-point peace plan to reconstruct Gaza amid the fragile ceasefire between Israel and Hamas in the region following over two years of conflict.

Leadership on the board includes the president, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, special envoy Steve Witkoff, former British Prime Minister Tony Blair, Work Bank President Ajay Banga, Trump’s son in law Jared Kushner and two billionaire investors: Robert Gabriel and Marc Rowan. Kushner, during the first meeting Thursday, was also designated as the “peace envoy.”

Representatives from roughly 40 nations attended the inaugural event. Trump also announced that the U.S. would commit $10 to the board’s mission.

data.title: Lead attorney in Trump tariff challenge lauds Supreme Court ruling
data.description:
The lead attorney who represented those suing over President Trump’s tariffs thanked the Supreme Court for striking down the plan, arguing the decision reflected the limits of the presidency.

The Supreme Court on Friday cast aside the bulk of Trump’s sweeping tariffs Friday, finding that he did not have the power to use an emergency statute to impose the taxes on foreign goods.

“Today, the U.S. Supreme Court stood up for the rule of law and Americans everywhere. Its message was simple: Presidents are powerful, but our Constitution is more powerful still. In America, only Congress can impose taxes on the American people,” Neal Katyal, the former Solicitor General under President Obama, said in a statement after the ruling.

“This case has always been about the presidency, not any one president. It has always been about separation of powers, and not the politics of the moment. I’m gratified to see our Supreme Court, which has been the bedrock of our government for 250 years, protect our most fundamental values.” 

The court’s ruling was a rare rejection of the White House stance, as the Supreme Court has routinely sided with Trump since he took office, often backing Trump’s claims of broad presidential authority.

But in the Friday ruling, the court determined Trump does not have the power to levy tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), a 1970s law that allows the president to regulate imports to respond to national emergencies that pose an “unusual and extraordinary” threat.   

“We claim no special competence in matters of economics or foreign affairs,” Chief Justice John Roberts wrote.

“We claim only, as we must, the limited role assigned to us by Article III of the Constitution. Fulfilling that role, we hold that IEEPA does not authorize the President to impose tariffs.”

data.title: Melania Trump donates inaugural gown to Smithsonian: 'This dress speaks with a distinct point of view'
data.description:
Melania Trump’s 2025 inaugural gown is getting immortalized at the Smithsonian, securing a spot at an exhibition recognizing the country’s first ladies.

“This is not a dress. This is more than 50 years of education, experience and wisdom realized with each thread,” she said at a Friday presentation ceremony at the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History in Washington.

Trump’s collaboration on the Hervé Pierre-designed, off-white dress with two striking bands of black silk gazar was featured heavily in her documentary film, “Melania.” The 55-year-old former model, who wore the outfit to President Trump’s second inaugural ball, opened up about the process, which she said she “relished,” in her remarks.

“This dress speaks with a distinct point of view,” Trump said, “bold and dignified and ruthlessly chic.”

The “black and white masterpiece,” she said, “showcases America’s pure spirit of originality, superior engineering and boundless creativity.”

In addition to the dress, a reproduction of the 1955 Harry Winston brooch Trump wore on Inauguration Day will be on display at the museum. The first lady borrowed the original jewelry and pinned it to a black ribbon that she sported as a choker at the inaugural festivities.

“I want to thank Mrs. Trump for helping us to add to this important first ladies collection,” Lonnie Bunch, Smithsonian Institution secretary, said. 

“Each first lady has left their mark, not just on the Whtie House, but on the nation. Each first lady reflects the time in which she served,” Bunch said.

The trip to the Smithsonian marked a return visit for Trump. In 2017, she donated the gown she sported at Donald Trump’s first inauguration to the same Smithsonian exhibition.

The museum’s first ladies-focused display includes 26 gowns. According to the Smithsonian, its aim is to encourage “visitors to consider the changing role played by the first lady and American women over the past 200 years.”
